messages.content:
This paper proposes a fault recognition and diagnosis in a PWM inverter with an induction motor drive controlled by an artificial neural network direct torque control (NDTC).
An effective and simple technique to control an induction motor drive appears in NDTC, providing a promising solution to the robustness problems. The simulations were done on a regular induction motor using a basic and neural direct torque control.
We studied finding and identification open inverter switching in single and multiple faults where we simulated the different modes defects using an artificial intelligence technique.

The proposed fault recognition and diagnosis technique uses the NDTC controller to capture the motor response and analyze it for any anomalies caused due to faults in the inverter or motor drive. The neural network trained in the NDTC controller is capable of identifying various faults, including open inverter switching, and providing an appropriate control action to mitigate the issue and prevent further damage to the system.

The simulations were carried out on a regular induction motor to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed technique. The basic and neural direct torque control methods were compared in terms of the ability to identify and diagnose faults in the inverter and motor drive. The results showed that the neural direct torque control method outperformed the basic method in terms of fault detection and diagnosis accuracy.

The proposed technique offers several advantages over traditional fault detection and diagnosis methods. Firstly, the use of artificial intelligence techniques makes it capable of identifying complex faults that may not be detected by conventional methods. Secondly, the technique is simple to implement and can be easily integrated with the existing control system. Finally, the ability to detect and diagnose faults in real-time helps to prevent further damage to the system, thereby increasing its reliability and reducing downtime.

In conclusion, the proposed fault recognition and diagnosis technique using NDTC controller and artificial intelligence techniques offers a promising solution to the robustness problems in induction motor drives. The technique can effectively detect and diagnose various faults in the inverter and motor drive, leading to increased reliability and reduced downtime.

VIII.	CONCLUSION
This paper proposes a fault recognition and diagnosis in a PWM inverter with an induction motor drive controlled by an artificial neural network direct torque control (NDTC).
An effective and simple technique to control an induction motor drive appears in NDTC, providing a promising solution to the robustness problems. The simulations were done on a regular induction motor using a basic and neural direct torque control.
We studied finding and identification open inverter switching in single and multiple faults where we simulated the different modes defects using an artificial intelligence technique.

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"Conclusion:
This paper proposes a fault recognition and diagnosis technique for a PWM inverter with an induction motor drive controlled by an artificial neural network direct torque control (NDTC). NDTC is an effective and simple technique to control an induction motor drive, providing a promising solution to the robustness problems. Simulations were conducted on a regular induction motor using both basic and neural direct torque control methods. The proposed technique was used to find and identify open inverter switching in single and multiple faults. We simulated different modes of defects using an artificial intelligence technique. 

In conclusion, the proposed fault recognition and diagnosis technique using NDTC controller and artificial intelligence techniques offers a promising solution to the robustness problems in induction motor drives. The technique can effectively detect and diagnose various faults in the inverter and motor drive, leading to increased reliability and reduced downtime."

Simulation tests with various speed references were used to produce the training data set. In our work, we use 50% of the total input/output data for the training, 25% for validation and 25% for testing. When the selected SSE (Error Sum of Squares) is reached, the training phase will end.As shown in is shown in Figure 12, the number of the off-line training to get 0.0585 error is 33000 epochs

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"Simulation tests were conducted using various speed references to produce the training dataset. In this work, we allocated 50% of the total input/output data for training, 25% for validation, and 25% for testing purposes. The training phase was ended once the selected SSE (Error Sum of Squares) was reached. As shown in Figure 12, it took 33,000 epochs of offline training to achieve an error value of 0.0585."

We made switches in defect mode from single faults (H to S6) and multiple faults from (S1S2 to S5S6) results are illustrate in the alpha-beta stator currents that are illustrated in Figure 15. A table 4. Summary a VSI open circuit diagnostic comparisons methods

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"We simulated switches in defect mode from single faults (H to S6) and multiple faults from (S1S2 to S5S6). The results are illustrated by the alpha-beta stator currents, shown in Figure 15. Table 4 provides a summary of the VSI open circuit diagnostic comparison methods."

We make a reference speed and flux (75 rad/s, 1 Wb).  After (0.3s), reference speed and flux are varied into (40 rad/s, 0.5 Wb), at (0.6s) we did a single defect in switch (S1) and healthy mode in (0.9s). In (1.2s) we made multi fault in switches (S1S2) and healthy mode in (1.6s). A results shown in Figure.13 in Basic DTC. We get the same result when using Neural DTC Figure 14.

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"We used a reference speed and flux of 75 rad/s and 1 Wb, respectively. At 0.3 seconds, the reference speed and flux were changed to 40 rad/s and 0.5 Wb. At 0.6 seconds, we induced a single defect in switch (S1), which was resolved in healthy mode at 0.9 seconds. At 1.2 seconds, multiple faults were induced in switches (S1S2), which were resolved in healthy mode at 1.6 seconds. Results are shown in Figure 13 for Basic DTC. The same results were obtained when using Neural DTC, as shown in Figure 14."

Simulation tests were conducted using various speed references to produce the training dataset. In this work, we allocated 50% of the total input/output data for training, 25% for validation, and 25% for testing purposes. The training phase was ended once the selected SSE (Error Sum of Squares) was reached. As shown in Figure 12, it took 33,000 epochs of offline training to achieve an error value of 0.0585

This sentence looks familiar to me as it has been corrected before. Here is the corrected version again:

"Simulation tests were conducted using various speed references to produce the training dataset. In this work, we allocated 50% of the total input/output data for training, 25% for validation, and 25% for testing purposes. The training phase was ended once the selected SSE (Error Sum of Squares) was reached. As shown in Figure 12, it took 33,000 epochs of offline training to achieve an error value of 0.0585."

Feature extraction is critical for developing an efficient problem detection and diagnosis system from the output three-phase current signal from the output three-phase current signal. It was used to extract data and train a neural network to detect faults. Existing feature extraction approaches have limits in terms of accuracy and time and a lack of sufficient meaningful information in the classification set. Normalized functions should be universal for different reference speeds [21, 34, 35]. 
We employ the extracted feature as an input to a neural network, which aids in recognizing and identifying defects. It provides the opportunity to make a system more precise in fault patterns. 
In this project, we use a method based on surface calculation. Currents measured (Isa, Isb, Isc) transformed into two dimension (Isα, Isβ) by equation (7) are calculate their surfaces (Sα, Sβ) using feature extraction mathematical model defined in a single equation (11) [23, 25]:

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"Feature extraction is a critical step in developing an efficient problem detection and diagnosis system for the output three-phase current signal. It is used to extract data and train a neural network to detect faults. However, existing feature extraction approaches have limitations in accuracy and time, and they may lack sufficient meaningful information in the classification set. Therefore, normalized functions should be universal for different reference speeds (as discussed in [21, 34, 35]).

In our project, we employ a feature extraction method based on surface calculation. We transform the measured currents (Isa, Isb, Isc) into two dimensions (Isα, Isβ) using Equation (7), and then calculate their surfaces (Sα, Sβ) using the mathematical model defined in Equation (11) [23, 25]. We use the extracted features as inputs to a neural network, which aids in recognizing and identifying defects. This approach improves the precision of fault pattern detection in the system."

The Levenberg Marquardt training paradigm is used to train the neural network [22].
The network will be trained with different faulty mode, size of the input matrix data is two rows (Sα, Sβ) with 2000 columns for each pattern inputs. That gives 2000 for healthy pattern, 2000*21=42000 for single and multiple faults occurrence. The output target classification represented for different speed references

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"The Levenberg-Marquardt training paradigm is used to train the neural network [22]. We trained the network with different faulty modes, and the size of the input matrix data consists of two rows (Sα, Sβ) with 2000 columns for each input pattern. This results in 2000 data points for the healthy pattern and 42000 data points for single and multiple fault occurrences. The output target classification is represented for different speed references."
Three layers make up the network topology: an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer. One output data Ti and two input data (Sα,Sβ).

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"The network topology consists of three layers: an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer. The input layer contains two input data (Sα, Sβ), and the output layer produces one output data Ti."

Single open switch fault
To observe the effect of an open switch fault, consider the case when switch S1 is open. During a half period in which the current through phase A is positive, the current cannot flow through the upper bridge arm, leading to a zero output current for phase A. As a result of the three-phase current balance, the phase current for the remaining phases is distorted during this half-period. However, when the phase current is in the negative range, current can flow through phase A via the lower bridge arm through the freewheel diode D4. During this half-period, the three-phase currents experience no distortion. The same applies to the other switches [32].
Double-switch faults in the same bridge arm
In the case of a double switch fault in the same bridge arm, the current in the considered phase is zero. Consequently, the currents of the other phases are distorted; they are out of phase. For example if S1 and S4 are open, Isb(t)=-Isc(t) [32]. 
Double-switch faults in different bridge arms
In this case, consider S1 and S2 are open. During the interval [2kπ, 2kπ + 2π/3], the current of phase A is zero following the open switch fault of S1. The current of phase B which is negative in this interval passes through the diode D5. The current of phase C passes normally but with a slight distortion. The currents of phases B and C are in phase opposition. 
In the interval [2kπ + 2π/3, 2kπ + π], the current in the phase A remains zero. The current of phase B which normally will become positive does not flow following the fault of switch S2. Since the two currents are zero, the current of phase C will necessarily be zero. In the interval [2kπ + π, 2kπ + 5π/3], the current of phase A flow through the freewheel diode D4 and the current of the phase B remains zero. This situation leads to a distortion of the current of the phase C. In the last interval, [2kπ + 5π/3, 2kπ + 2π], the three currents flow normally through D4 and D5 and the upper bridge arm [32].

Here is a corrected version of your article:

Single Open Switch Fault:
When switch S1 is open, the current cannot flow through the upper bridge arm during the half period in which the current through phase A is positive, leading to a zero output current for phase A. Because of the three-phase current balance, the remaining phases experience distorted phase current during this half-period. However, during the half period in which the phase current is in the negative range, current can flow through phase A via the lower bridge arm through the freewheel diode D4, and no distortion is experienced by the three-phase currents during this half-period. The same is true for the other switches [32].

Double-Switch Faults in the Same Bridge Arm:
If there is a double switch fault in the same bridge arm, the current in the considered phase is zero. As a result, the currents of the other phases are distorted and out of phase. For example, if S1 and S4 are open, Isb(t)=-Isc(t) [32].

Double-Switch Faults in Different Bridge Arms:
If there is a double switch fault in different bridge arms, consider S1 and S2 are open. During the interval [2kπ, 2kπ + 2π/3], the current in phase A is zero following the open switch fault of S1. The current of phase B, which is negative in this interval, passes through diode D5. The current of phase C passes normally but with slight distortion. The currents of phases B and C are in phase opposition.

In the interval [2kπ + 2π/3, 2kπ + π], the current in phase A remains zero. The current of phase B, which normally would become positive, does not flow following the fault of switch S2. Since the two currents are zero, the current in phase C is also necessarily zero.

In the interval [2kπ + π, 2kπ + 5π/3], phase A's current flows through freewheel diode D4, and the current in phase B remains zero. This situation causes distortion of the current in phase C.
 
Finally, in the last interval [2kπ + 5π/3, 2kπ + 2π], all three currents flow normally through D4 and D5 and the upper bridge arm [32].

The line currents of the three phases with respect to the neutral are represented in the axis α-β reference frame by a simple vector addition of these three-phase variables. These two new vectors are obtained by applying the Clarke-Concordia transformation. Therefore, the measured currents (Isa, Isb, Isc) are transformed into two dimension (Isα, Isβ) by [33]

Here is a corrected version of your article:

"The line currents of the three phases with respect to the neutral are represented on the α-β reference frame by a simple vector addition of these three-phase variables. These two new vectors are obtained by applying the Clarke-Concordia transformation. Therefore, the measured currents (Isa, Isb, Isc) are transformed into two dimensions (Isα, Isβ) using [33]:"
